Repairing Hail and Wind Damage on a Springfield Commercial Roof

html:
Documenting Hail Damage on a Springfield Commercial Roof

Greene County sits inside one of the more active hail corridors in the Ozarks, and commercial roofs here take hits that don't always announce themselves. A hailstorm that leaves obvious dents in parked cars can leave a low-slope membrane roof looking almost untouched from the ground — the damage is up on the field, not visible from the parking lot, which is exactly why a lot of hail claims start with an owner who didn't know there was anything to file for.

Where Hail Damage Actually Shows Up

On a single-ply membrane roof — TPO or PVC, both common across Springfield's newer distribution and retail construction — hail impact often shows as fractured or dislodged granules on modified bitumen caps, or as bruising you can feel more than see on the membrane itself. On standing seam and R-panel metal roofs, which cover a good share of the manufacturing and warehouse stock along the I-44 corridor, hail leaves visible dents in the pan and can crack the paint finish, opening the door to corrosion later even if the panel itself hasn't failed structurally.

Soft metal is where we start regardless of the primary roof system. Pipe boots, HVAC curb caps, gutter aprons, and vent covers dent from hail at roughly the same energy that a membrane needs to actually take damage, which makes them a reliable indicator of storm severity across the whole roof even before we've mapped every field impact.

How We Measure and Record It

We test soft metal at a grid of points across the roof, across the whole field rather than only wherever damage looks worst, and we photograph each impact with a location reference tied to a roof plan. Where hail has fractured granules on a modified bitumen or built-up cap sheet, we note the density of impacts per test square rather than describing it in general terms — that specificity is what lets an adjuster verify our findings against their own inspection instead of taking our word for it.

Older membranes complicate this. A fifteen-year-old EPDM field with UV-related surface cracking can look similar to hail bruising to an untrained eye, and we separate the two explicitly in our documentation rather than letting age-related wear get folded into the storm claim. That distinction is part of what makes a hail damage report credible instead of something an adjuster discounts on sight.

Metal Roofs Need a Different Read

Standing seam and R-panel systems common on Springfield's newer commercial and industrial buildings show hail differently than membrane. We measure dent depth and frequency across representative panel sections, check seams and ribs for stress cracking near dent sites, and note whether the finish coating has fractured — because a cracked finish over a dented panel is a corrosion problem waiting to start, even on a roof that's still watertight today.

Building the Claim-Ready Report

The finished documentation package ties dated photos, soft-metal measurements, and a written scope together into something an adjuster can work from without a second site visit. We don't estimate what a hail claim is worth before that inspection happens, and we don't tell an owner their claim is guaranteed — we build the record of what's actually on the roof and let the policy and adjuster take it from there.

We're your roofing contractor, not a public adjuster — we document and substantiate hail damage so you and your adjuster are working from the same accurate scope. For the full claim process beyond hail specifically, see our commercial roof insurance claims page, or if the storm brought high winds along with the hail, our wind and storm damage page covers that side of the inspection.

Hail Damage Claim FAQ

Can hail damage a roof without leaving visible signs from the ground?

Yes, this is common on low-slope membrane and metal roofs. Damage often only shows up on close inspection of the field and soft metal, which is why we recommend an inspection after any significant hail event even if nothing looks obviously wrong from below.

How do you tell hail damage from normal roof wear?

We compare impact patterns, density, and location against what age-related wear typically looks like on that membrane type, and we document the two separately rather than blending them into one finding.

Does a few dented soft-metal fixtures mean the whole roof is damaged?

Not necessarily, but it tells us the storm had enough energy to cause damage, which is why we use soft metal as a starting indicator and then inspect the full field before drawing conclusions.

Will a hail claim cover a full roof replacement or just repairs?

That depends on the extent and density of the damage relative to the roof's remaining life, which is something we document but the insurer and policy ultimately determine.

How soon after a hailstorm should the roof be inspected?

As soon as reasonably possible. Hail damage that goes unaddressed can let moisture into a compromised membrane before the claim is even filed, and dated documentation closest to the event is the most credible record.
